header.title: Daily FI Tape
header.as_of_note: NY close briefing; Asia and pre-market update remains separate.

bullets: Oil fell again and equities rallied, but Treasuries gave back part of the prior rally as markets still priced a restrictive Fed, ECB spillover risk, and unresolved energy pass-through.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Oil fell again and equities rallied, but Treasuries gave back part of the prior rally as markets still priced a restrictive Fed, ECB spillover risk, and unresolved energy pass-through.

top_drivers.title: Oil relief no longer guaranteed a Treasury rally
top_drivers.short_summary: WTI and Brent fell more than 3%, but UST2Y, UST5Y, and UST10Y all rose as policy markets still respected inflation risk.
top_drivers.detail: UST10Y closed near 4.48%, up 3 bp, while BE10Y rose 2 bp to 231 bp.

top_drivers.title: Europe became the inflation-policy test case
top_drivers.short_summary: The ECB had raised rates in response to the energy shock and officials warned that pass-through was broadening beyond fuel.
top_drivers.detail: European duration could rally on Hormuz headlines, but the policy path still included additional hike risk.

top_drivers.title: Liquidity stress was visible in Indonesia
top_drivers.short_summary: Indonesian bond funds delayed redemptions because local bond-market liquidity had deteriorated after the selloff.
top_drivers.detail: This was direct liquidity evidence, unlike a simple Treasury yield move.

latest_asia_premarket.summary: Separately, checked Asia/pre-market pricing before the Taipei handoff shows 10Y Treasury note futures 109.52 (-0.24%); 30Y Treasury bond futures 112.38 (-0.44%); S&P 500 futures 7,435 (0%); Nasdaq 100 futures 29,662 (0%); WTI crude futures 84.88 (0%).

items.title: 10Y Treasury note futures
items.detail: 109.52, -0.24% from the prior close. Treasury futures give the cleanest early read on whether the duration bid held after the NY close

items.title: 30Y Treasury bond futures
items.detail: 112.38, -0.44% from the prior close. Long-bond futures help separate a persistent long-end bid from a one-session cash-market move

items.title: S&P 500 futures
items.detail: 7,435, 0% from the prior close. Equity futures show whether the overnight handoff confirmed or faded the NY risk tone

items.title: Nasdaq 100 futures
items.detail: 29,662, 0% from the prior close. Nasdaq futures are the early check on whether growth and technology beta stabilized

items.title: WTI crude futures
items.detail: 84.88, 0% from the prior close. WTI futures test whether the oil channel was still easing inflation pressure by the Taipei handoff

watch_events.title: Fed path into Warsh meeting
watch_events.why_it_matters: Economists pushed cuts into 2027 while traders still priced meaningful hike risk.

watch_events.title: ECB second-round inflation
watch_events.why_it_matters: European officials were watching services and core pass-through from the energy shock.

watch_events.title: Indonesia liquidity and EM policy
watch_events.why_it_matters: Delayed redemptions and currency pressure made EM liquidity a live fixed-income risk.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: WTI was -3.23% on the day and -6.25% over one week; Brent moved in the same direction, pulling inflation compensation lower and reinforcing Treasury demand.

cross_asset_cards.title: Equities
cross_asset_cards.summary: SPX was +0.5%, NDX +0.64%, and SOX +1.52%; the equity tape leaned tech-beta downside rather than broad risk-appetite confirmation.

cross_asset_cards.title: Credit
cross_asset_cards.summary: LQD was -0.06%, HYG 0%, and EMB +0.09%; these are ETF total returns, not direct OAS or CDX. With Treasury yields lower, duration should have helped bond ETF prices, so flat or negative HY/EM returns argue for weak spread confirmation rather than a broad credit rally.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: DXY was -0.11% while gold was +3.05%, consistent with lower yields and limited haven stress.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening fixed-income thesis

The June 12 close was not another straightforward oil-relief duration rally. Oil did fall again: WTI ended near 84.88, down 3.23%, and Brent near 87.33, down 3.37%. Equities liked that move, with SPX up 0.50%, NDX up 0.64%, SOX up 1.52%, and VIX down 9.05%. But Treasuries gave back some of the prior day's rally. UST2Y rose 4 bp to 4.09%, UST5Y rose 3 bp to 4.21%, UST10Y rose 3 bp to 4.48%, and UST30Y rose 2 bp to 4.97%. BE10Y rose 2 bp to 231 bp. The market was saying that lower oil helped risk appetite, but it did not fully remove the restrictive-policy problem.

The day's news flow explains the split. European and US bonds had rallied on hopes for progress toward a Middle East deal, but by the New York close the US curve was less clean. The ECB had just raised rates for the first time since 2023 in response to inflation caused by the Iran war. ECB officials were saying the rise in oil and gas had begun to pass through into other consumer prices, especially services, even if wages had not yet generated a second-round effect. That made June 12 different from a simple commodity move. Lower crude prices were helpful, but central banks were already reacting to inflation that had accumulated before the latest drop.

The Fed path also stayed tight. Economists had pushed expected Fed cuts into 2027, while investors still priced meaningful hike risk. A Goldman rates trader said markets were fairly pricing the Fed path on a probability-adjusted basis given resilient growth, higher prices, and the corporate AI spending boom. That point matters for the rates section: if the economy is resilient and inflation is sticky, lower oil for one day does not automatically restore a rate-cut story. The front end can remain heavy even as equities rally.

Credit was better but selective. HYG and JNK were essentially flat to slightly higher, EMB rose 0.09%, and LQD slipped 0.06%. Those moves were mild compared with the equity rally. Credit investors gave up some wartime hedges on European companies, but the day also carried a direct liquidity warning from Indonesia: funds holding Indonesian bonds delayed redemptions because local bond liquidity had deteriorated after the selloff. That is important. June 12 had real risk-on elements, but it also had evidence that parts of EM fixed income were not functioning normally.

The summary should therefore be balanced. Lower oil supported equities, European risk assets, and parts of credit. It did not produce a clean Treasury bull rally because the Fed and ECB still faced inflation pass-through, and it did not erase liquidity stress in EM local bonds. The day was a test of whether markets could look through an energy shock before central banks were ready to do so. The answer at the close was only partial.

## Rates, curve, and Fed pricing

Treasury pricing on June 12 was the key reason the note cannot be written as another relief rally. UST2Y rose 4 bp, UST5Y rose 3 bp, UST10Y rose 3 bp, and UST30Y rose 2 bp. The 2s10s curve flattened 1 bp to about 39 bp, while 2s30s narrowed 2 bp to about 88 bp. The move was not violent, but it was important because it came with oil down more than 3%. The market was not ignoring the oil decline. It was saying the oil decline was not enough to remove the Fed's inflation risk.

The Fed facts were explicit. Surveyed economists expected the Fed to hold rates at the first Warsh-led meeting and pushed cuts into 2027. Investors still saw hike risk. Goldman rates commentary argued that traders were fairly pricing the Fed path given resilient activity, higher prices, and corporate spending. That framework helps reconcile the price action. A lower oil price can reduce the chance of an immediate inflation accident, but if the economy remains resilient and price pressure is already broad, the Fed still has little reason to validate a dovish curve rally.

Breakevens also resisted a pure disinflation read. BE10Y rose 2 bp to 231 bp even as WTI and Brent fell sharply. TIPS10Y rose 1 bp to 2.17%. That means both inflation compensation and real yields moved a little higher. The market was not pricing an oil-driven collapse in inflation expectations. It was pricing a still-restrictive policy setting in which the Fed would watch whether the energy shock filtered into core prices, services, wages, and expectations. The month-to-date move stayed meaningful: UST10Y was still down 7 bp on the week but only 2 bp higher on the month, while TIPS10Y remained 18 bp above the prior month.

Europe made the Fed path harder to dismiss. The ECB had raised rates to 2.25% and officials were already discussing whether another move could come as soon as July or September. Emmanuel Moulin said the energy shock had begun to pass into parts of the consumer basket, especially services. Nagel and other officials emphasized the need to respond if core pressure broadened. European bonds could rally on deal headlines, but policy remained hawkish because the inflation shock had already entered the data. US rates had to price that global precedent.

The Czech central bank added another example. Headline inflation was below target at 2.1%, yet the governor said the case for a June hike had strengthened because domestic money supply, household credit, and the fiscal deficit were adding upside risks to core inflation. That detail is useful because it shows why central banks were not simply responding to spot oil. They were weighing whether the shock would interact with domestic credit and fiscal conditions. For June 12, the rates conclusion is that the Treasury curve had room to sell off even with oil lower because the policy debate had moved from headline energy to second-round risk.

## Supply, term premium, and liquidity

June 12 had stronger direct liquidity evidence than June 10 or June 11. Two funds holding Indonesian bonds delayed redemptions because liquidity in the local bond market had deteriorated after the selloff and rupiah volatility. A Korean manager extended the redemption cycle because of temporary difficulties selling bonds. That is actual market-function evidence. It does not require inference from a yield move. It says that the prior EM selloff had impaired liquidity enough to affect investor redemption terms.

That Indonesian liquidity stress belongs in the fixed-income thesis because it changes how the market should read EM carry and local duration. Indonesia had been trying to restore confidence through policy reassurance, but if funds cannot sell bonds easily, higher yields are not just valuation. They are also compensation for liquidity and currency risk. The delayed-redemption fact therefore sits next to Bank Indonesia's earlier willingness to let yields rise to defend the rupiah. Policy credibility, liquidity, and FX were all tied together.

China supplied a different liquidity lesson. Weak loan demand had produced a liquidity glut that dragged down borrowing costs and distorted financial markets. That is the mirror image of Indonesia: not a shortage of liquidity, but a surplus caused by weak credit demand. The important point was that financial-market distortions across Asia were triggering interventions from Korea to China. For a global FI tape, that is useful because it prevents a one-size-fits-all EM conclusion. Some markets needed higher yields to attract capital; others had loose conditions that risked asset distortions.

Primary supply was active but not the same as Treasury supply. Lloyds sold yen-denominated Samurai bonds into a market where issuance was strong and Japanese buyers liked shorter maturities and wider spreads. Metinvest tested investor demand after redeeming a bond. Jakarta was considering municipal issuance. These are credit and regional funding stories, not UST auction stories. The day's most direct market-function evidence came from Indonesian liquidity stress and Asian financial distortions, while global corporate and sovereign issuance stayed active but selective.

India's fiscal and subsidy discussion also belonged in the supply frame. Officials were considering allowing the fiscal gap to widen toward 4.8% of GDP rather than the 4.3% target because higher energy prices raised pressure on fuel and fertilizer subsidies. Indian bonds briefly gave up gains as the 10-year yield edged higher. That fact tied oil directly to sovereign borrowing needs. It was not a US Treasury driver, but it showed why global term premium could remain sensitive to energy even when crude was falling on the day.

Samurai and municipal issuance showed that local funding channels were still active. Lloyds sold yen bonds into strong Japanese demand, while Jakarta continued working toward a municipal-bond framework after large budget-transfer cuts. Metinvest explored whether investor demand would allow a return to market after using cash to redeem debt. These examples are not interchangeable, but together they show that borrowers were testing windows rather than assuming liquidity was universal. Strong markets had buyers; stressed or policy-dependent borrowers still needed careful pricing.

The liquidity comparison between Indonesia and China was especially useful. Indonesia's issue was insufficient tradability after outflows, which forced funds to delay redemptions. China's issue was weak loan demand and abundant liquidity, which pulled borrowing costs lower and risked distortions. Both situations can create fixed-income instability. One comes from a shortage of balance-sheet willingness, the other from money looking for assets in a weak economy. June 12's global note should keep both in view because they show why lower oil does not create a single global liquidity condition.

India added a fiscal dimension to that liquidity story. Officials were considering allowing the fiscal gap to widen to as much as 4.8% of GDP, which would put more pressure on bond supply just as energy and currency risks were keeping central banks cautious. That is not the same as U.S. Treasury supply, but it matters for global duration because investors compare fiscal paths across markets. If EM sovereigns need more borrowing room while global rates remain high, local curves require higher compensation even when headline oil is falling.

Primary-market activity also stayed selective. Lloyds sold ¥75 billion of Samurai bonds, foreign issuers continued to tap Japan, banks lined up about €1.7 billion of debt for Triton's Flender acquisition, and Ukraine's steel producer tested investor demand after a bond redemption. Each deal had its own sponsorship and structure. The common signal was that funding markets were not closed, but borrowers still needed the right currency, collateral, buyer base, or refinancing purpose. That is a very different message from a broad easing in financial conditions.

## Credit conditions

Credit conditions improved at the surface but stayed highly discriminating. HYG was flat, JNK rose 0.02%, EMB rose 0.09%, and LQD slipped 0.06%. That is a stable credit ETF tape, not a major spread rally. Equities and volatility were much more enthusiastic: SOX rose 1.52%, NDX 0.64%, SPX 0.50%, and VIX fell 9.05%. Credit therefore lagged equities. The better interpretation is that lower oil reduced hedging demand and helped risk appetite, but lenders still cared about borrower structure.

European credit investors gave up some wartime hedges as oil fell and hopes for a deal improved. That was a real positive. But the same day carried caution from Pimco's Dan Ivascyn that complex credit structures were beginning to resemble pre-crisis buildup patterns. The point is not that another crisis was imminent. It is that the expansion of direct lending, structured lending, and AI-linked financing made borrower quality and documentation more important. A lower VIX day does not eliminate those risks.

Samurai issuance and Japanese buyer demand showed that high-quality borrowers still had access. Lloyds sold ¥75 billion of yen bonds, with demand supported by short maturities and wider spreads in a rising-rate environment. That is constructive for bank credit access. Citigroup had also issued high-grade debt in the broader week, and Oracle bond spreads had tightened after management calmed investors about future issuance. These examples show that the market was not closed to credible issuers with transparent funding plans.

Weaker or more complex borrowers faced a different market. Travelodge had been downgraded deeper into junk and bearish positioning in its debt had increased because of refinancing concerns. Indonesian local bond funds were delaying redemptions. India had fiscal slippage risk if oil raised subsidy costs. Brazil inflation was above target before its policy meeting. These facts all matter for credit because they show how higher rates and energy volatility move from macro variables into borrower cash flow, fiscal support, and refinancing access.

The credit conclusion for June 12 is therefore not broad risk-on. It is selective normalization. Lower oil allowed hedges to come off and helped equities, but credit remained more disciplined than stocks. High-quality bank and corporate issuers could place debt. More leveraged, less liquid, or policy-exposed credits still had to pay for uncertainty. That distinction should guide every reference to credit ETF returns in the report.

European credit hedges coming off was the most positive spread-market signal. As oil fell and talk of a Hormuz deal improved, investors reduced wartime protection on European companies. That is a direct credit-risk signal, unlike an ETF total return. It says the market saw a lower probability of immediate energy disruption and a lower need to pay for corporate default protection tied to that scenario. The limitation is that hedges can come off quickly before fundamental credit risk has actually improved.

Pimco's warning on complex credit structures kept the credit conclusion from becoming too bullish. Rapid growth in direct lending and structured credit can look stable in a benign tape, but the risk appears when refinancing, valuations, or collateral assumptions are tested. June 12 had exactly the kind of mixed setup that makes that warning relevant: equities rallied and oil fell, yet the Fed and ECB were still restrictive and EM liquidity stress was visible. That combination can reward carry while hiding liquidity risk until redemptions or refinancing dates force it into view.

Borrower-specific stress remained present. Travelodge debt was under pressure after a downgrade and rising short interest, and Metinvest was testing whether investors would finance a return to the market after earlier restructuring discussions. India had subsidy and fiscal-risk questions. These do not all belong in the same spread bucket, but they share a common macro sensitivity: higher rates and volatile input costs reduce the margin for weak balance sheets. Lower oil helps, but the borrower still has to prove cash flow and market access.

High-quality issuance was more resilient. Lloyds could use the Samurai market because Japanese buyers valued the spread and maturity profile. Oracle-style discipline around funding was being rewarded in credit even when equity narratives were mixed. Credit conditions were selective, not broadly easy. The market was comfortable financing recognizable names and structures, while weaker balance sheets and less liquid markets still needed a risk premium.

Borrower-level credit news kept the risk rally from looking uniform. SpaceX was moving toward the largest IPO of all time and shadow markets were pricing a strong debut, but that equity story did not erase balance-sheet questions elsewhere. Ferrari's electric-vehicle launch had raised strategic doubts among investors, and the share-price reaction showed that even premium issuers can lose their equity cushion when a product transition looks uncertain. For credit, that matters because equity volatility changes refinancing flexibility before it changes reported leverage.

The leveraged-finance pipeline also required discrimination. Triton's Flender debt package benefited from institutional demand for new-money deals, while other borrowers were still navigating maturity extensions, restructuring, or governance concerns. The stable ETF tape therefore understated the actual underwriting work. Investors were not simply adding credit beta because oil fell. They were choosing between sponsor-backed deals, strategic issuers, EM sovereign or municipal supply, and companies whose business models had become more sensitive to rates or energy.

## Global duration, EM hard-currency credit, and cross-market confirmation

Global duration confirmed the two-sided nature of the US rates close. European and US bonds had rallied on renewed hopes for an Iran deal, with UK two-year yields down sharply and German two-year yields falling nearly 10 bp during the European session. But the US close still showed Treasury yields up on the day. That divergence matters. Europe was reacting to the immediate oil headline after the ECB had already hiked; the US market was still looking ahead to Warsh, resilient growth, and whether the Fed would validate or resist lower-hike pricing.

The ECB became the central global rates story. It raised borrowing costs for the first time since 2023 and warned that inflation triggered by the Iran war was widening through the economy. Some investors argued the ECB would later need to reverse course because tighter policy would slow growth; swaps still priced additional hikes over the coming year. This tension was important for global duration. It created opportunities in short-dated European government bonds for investors who believed the market had over-priced hikes, but it also showed how hard it was for central banks to ignore energy pass-through.

EM policy was equally varied. The Czech Republic was considering a hike despite below-target headline inflation because domestic credit and fiscal conditions could create core pressure. Brazil's inflation was above the top of its target band. Indonesia had liquidity stress and currency pressure. India faced possible fiscal slippage because higher energy prices could raise subsidies. These stories did not move in one direction, but they all made clear that the oil shock was interacting with domestic balance sheets and policy credibility.

Cross-asset confirmation was constructive but not complete. Equities rose, VIX fell, oil dropped, and the dollar softened. Gold rose more than 3%, however, showing that some investors still wanted protection against unresolved geopolitical risk or policy uncertainty. The semiconductor rally and travel-stock gains were consistent with lower oil and improved risk appetite. The gold move reminded readers that the deal path was not fully trusted. That is why the global read was relief, not resolution.

Hard-currency credit and EM assets therefore needed a country-by-country filter. Some sovereign and quasi-sovereign borrowers could benefit from lower oil and better risk appetite. Others still faced liquidity, fiscal, or currency constraints. The market was no longer in full escalation mode, but the fixed-income implications remained uneven: developed-market central banks were debating pass-through, EM local markets were dealing with liquidity and capital flows, and credit investors were separating reliable issuers from fragile balance sheets.

The European rates move was one of the strongest non-US confirmations. UK and German two-year yields fell sharply on deal optimism, but the ECB's prior hike and official commentary kept the front-end debate alive. JPMorgan Asset Management, UBS, and RBC BlueBay argued that markets might be pricing too many ECB hikes and underestimating the chance of a later U-turn. That means European duration had two forces at once: short-term inflation pass-through pushing rates higher, and growth damage from tightening creating future rally potential. June 12 carried both forces.

The Czech case made the central-bank dilemma more concrete. Headline inflation was below target, yet policymakers were considering a hike because domestic money supply, credit, and fiscal dynamics could push core inflation higher. That is a more sophisticated policy problem than oil alone. It tells the reader that central banks were not just reacting to front-month crude. They were deciding whether the energy shock would interact with domestic credit creation and fiscal deficits. That same question was relevant for the Fed and ECB.

Cross-asset confirmation was also divided by asset class. Travel and leisure equities rallied because lower oil directly improved margins, and semiconductors rose with the broader risk tone. Gold rose more than 3%, however, which signaled that investors still wanted protection. The dollar weakened, but not enough to suggest a full unwind of Fed premium. The market therefore confirmed relief through equities and volatility, while rates and gold showed that policy and geopolitical insurance were still priced.

For EM hard-currency credit, the better oil tape helped but did not settle fiscal questions. India's possible deficit widening, Indonesia's redemption delays, Brazil's above-target inflation, and Turkey's high policy-rate stance all showed that EM credits were managing domestic constraints. Lower oil is a tailwind for importers and for countries exposed to fuel subsidies. It is not an automatic spread-compression event if liquidity, fiscal, or inflation credibility is impaired.

The central-bank distribution was unusually wide. ECB officials Dolenc, Nagel, Moulin, and Kazimir were all still focused on inflation pass-through, while the Czech central bank chief called a June hike a real possibility. Brazil's inflation had jumped above the target limit before its rate meeting. India, in contrast, had inflation below target, supporting the RBI's decision to hold. That cross-section matters because it explains why global duration could not trade as one block. Oil relief helped, but local inflation regimes were diverging.

The Asia distortion story also belonged in the global section. Korea's large equity winners had made its market unusually concentrated, while China's weak loan demand had produced excess liquidity and downward pressure on borrowing costs. These distortions were triggering intervention-style responses. For U.S. fixed income, the read is that global liquidity was uneven rather than simply abundant. Some markets had too little bond liquidity, some had too much bank liquidity, and some had policy rates still moving higher. That made the U.S. close less clean than the oil price alone suggested.

## Market implications and watchpoints

The first watchpoint was whether oil relief would keep moving into breakevens. On June 12, WTI and Brent fell sharply, yet BE10Y rose 2 bp. That tells the reader to watch inflation compensation rather than crude alone. If oil keeps falling and breakevens follow, the Fed-hike debate should soften. If oil falls but breakevens stay sticky, the market is saying second-round effects or resilient demand are more important than the front-month commodity move.

The second watchpoint was Warsh's Fed. Economists pushed cuts into 2027, traders still priced hike risk, and the first Warsh-led meeting was approaching. The communication problem mattered because a less forward-guided Fed could leave rates markets more volatile around each inflation or oil headline. A clear statement that the Fed would look through energy could extend the risk rally. A statement that inflation expectations were at risk could put the front end back under pressure.

The third watchpoint was Europe. ECB officials were already discussing additional tightening even as investors debated whether the market had over-priced hikes. If European inflation data continued to broaden beyond energy, US duration would have less room to rally on lower oil alone. If growth weakness became dominant, European short-end rallies could become the path other markets followed. This was the main global rates fork.

The fourth watchpoint was liquidity. Indonesian bond fund redemptions were delayed because the local market had become hard to trade. That is the kind of stress that can survive a better global equity day. If EM liquidity normalizes and the rupiah stabilizes, the global FI tape can treat the oil decline as a relief impulse. If redemption pressure or currency defense deepens, the market will need to price a separate EM funding premium.

A fifth watchpoint was whether credit hedges stayed unwound. If oil kept falling and European companies no longer needed wartime protection, spread markets could continue to normalize. But if crude rebounded or the Hormuz talks stalled, hedges would likely return before cash bonds had time to adjust. The speed of the hedge market made it an early warning signal for the next session.

A sixth watchpoint was sovereign fiscal sensitivity to oil. India, Brazil, Turkey, and parts of Europe were all showing different versions of the same problem: energy prices affect inflation, subsidies, fiscal balances, and external accounts. If lower oil persisted, those pressures would ease in different speeds across countries. If oil rose again, the most vulnerable credits would be those where fiscal or currency buffers were already thin.

A final watchpoint was whether liquidity stress stayed localized. Indonesian fund redemption delays could remain an idiosyncratic local-market problem, or they could become a sign that EM investors were no longer willing to warehouse duration through volatile FX moves. The next confirmation would come from rupiah stability, 10-year Indonesian yields, and whether other EM funds reported similar difficulties. Until then, June 12 should be treated as a relief day with a visible liquidity warning attached.

The third watchpoint was whether liquidity problems would migrate from local markets into hard-currency credit. Indonesia's delayed redemptions were a local-bond problem, but they matter for EMB-style risk because the same investors often manage local currency, hard-currency, and FX exposure together. If local liquidity stress forces risk reduction, hard-currency spreads can widen even when U.S. Treasuries are stable. Conversely, if official reassurance and lower oil restore liquidity, EM hard-currency credit can outperform without needing a major Treasury rally.

The fourth watchpoint was central-bank sequencing. The ECB had already hiked, the Fed was approaching a Warsh-led meeting, Brazil and Czech policy risk were live, and India was on a different path. A single oil headline could not settle that distribution. The next durable fixed-income move would need confirmation from breakevens, real yields, and front-end pricing across several markets, not just another drop in WTI or Brent.

methodology_note: Original market synthesis from checked prices and fixed-income news developments.
